basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Nūḥ — “Noah” — bears the name of the prophet of long patience. The whole sūra is the first-person account of a tireless and almost wholly refused call: Noah calls his people “night and day,” aloud and in secret, and his call “only increases their flight.” At the heart of this drama of obstinacy, a pearl of Qushayrī lights the way: the asking of forgiveness (istighfār) is the knocker at the doors of grace; whoever has a need with God will reach his goal only by first presenting his asking of forgiveness; whoever wants the favour, let him take the way of acknowledging the fault. The sūra then unfolds the signs of creation — the seven superposed heavens, the moon as light and the sun as a lamp, man “grown from the earth like plants” — then closes on Noah's lament and his twofold prayer: for the believers, forgiveness; and the handing over of the fate of the obstinate into God's hands. Qushayrī notes that Noah, knowing his people would not believe, nonetheless conveyed the message: he showed that guidance is not in his power — hearts are in Your hands, O You alone.

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ أَنْ أَنذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ
versets.tr: innā arsalnā nūḥan ilā qawmihi an andhir qawmaka min qabli an yaʾtiyahum ʿadhābun alīmun
versets.trad: Yes, We sent Noah to his people: “Warn your people before a painful chastisement reaches them!”
versets.commentaire: arsalnā nūḥan (أَرْسَلْنا نوحًا): We sent Noah. Qushayrī notes at once a theological subtlety: “the sending of messengers is a favour (faḍl) of God; He has the right, by His pure sovereignty, to do what He wills, and the sending of messengers was no obligation upon Him.” The mission is no due but a grace. And God sends even to those He knows will not believe — that none may have an excuse, and that the warning may always precede the chastisement.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / أ ل م

versets.ar: قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: qāla yā qawmi innī lakum nadhīrun mubīnun
versets.trad: He said: “O my people! I am for you a clear warner.
versets.commentaire: nadhīr mubīn (نَذير مُبين): a clear warner. Noah presents himself not as a judge but as a servant charged with a clear message. Qushayrī recalls that he already knew the hardness of his people — “he knew of them that they would not accept” — and yet he conveyed it. This is the prophet's greatness: to warn clearly, without unloading his task onto the outcome. The clarity of the message does not guarantee its acceptance; it only removes all excuse from the one who refuses.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ق و م
ن ذ ر / ب ي ن

versets.ar: أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ
versets.tr: ani ʿbudū llāha wa-ttaqūhu wa-aṭīʿūni
versets.trad: Worship God, fear Him, and obey me!
versets.commentaire: Three linked commands: worship (ʿbudū), fear (ttaqūhu), obey (aṭīʿūni). The order is not indifferent: worship is the goal, reverent fear the disposition of the heart, and obedience to the messenger the concrete way. To follow the prophet is not to cling to a man but to enter by the door he points to: he asks for obedience only to lead to God, not to himself.

versets.ar: يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ إِذَا جَاءَ لَا يُؤَخَّرُ لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: yaghfir lakum min dhunūbikum wa-yuʾakhkhirkum ilā ajalin musamman inna ajala llāhi idhā jāʾa lā yuʾakhkharu law kuntum taʿlamūna
versets.trad: He will forgive you your sins and grant you respite until an appointed term; but when God's appointed term comes, it cannot be deferred. Ah, would that you knew!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī dwells on the min of “your sins”: it marks the genus, not a part — God forgives faults. And he notes the delicacy: Noah does not promise them forgiveness of what they are about to commit, for that would encourage them to evil. Forgiveness is offered, and with it a respite (taʾkhīr) — a reprieve to amend. But the term (ajal) fixed by God, when it comes, is not deferred: the mercy of the respite has its limit, and wisdom is to profit from it before it closes.

mots.racine: أ ج ل / س م و

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِي لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا
versets.tr: qāla rabbi innī daʿawtu qawmī laylan wa-nahāran
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! I have called my people night and day,
versets.commentaire: daʿawtu qawmī laylan wa-nahāran (دَعَوْتُ قَوْمي لَيْلًا وَنهارًا): I have called my people night and day. Noah turns to God and reports his sorrow. “Night and day”: without respite, without an hour's pause. It is the portrait of the caller who spares himself nothing, who spends all his strength. And yet — the sequel will say it — the most total effort does not force the closed heart. Noah learns, and teaches us, that perseverance is our part, but guidance remains God's.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ر ب ب
د ع و / ق و م
ل ي ل / ن ه ر

versets.ar: فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعَائِي إِلَّا فِرَارًا
versets.tr: fa-lam yazidhum duʿāʾī illā firāran
versets.trad: but my call only increased their flight.
versets.commentaire: lam yazidhum… illā firāran (لَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ إلّا فِرارًا): it increased them in nothing but flight. A painful paradox: the more Noah calls, the more they flee. Qushayrī draws the decisive lesson: “he showed that guidance is not in his power, and said: if You will their faith, their hearts are in Your power, glory to You.” The faithful caller does not measure his success by the outcome: he sows, and leaves the harvest to God. The refusal of others does not discourage him, it brings him back to his Lord.

versets.ar: وَإِنِّي كُلَّمَا دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوا أَصَابِعَهُمْ فِي آذَانِهِمْ وَاسْتَغْشَوْا ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا اسْتِكْبَارًا
versets.tr: wa-innī kullamā daʿawtuhum li-taghfira lahum jaʿalū aṣābiʿahum fī ādhānihim wa-staghshaw thiyābahum wa-aṣarrū wa-stakbarū stikbāran
versets.trad: Each time I called them so that You might forgive them, they thrust their fingers into their ears, wrapped themselves in their garments, persisted, and grew most arrogant.
versets.commentaire: A striking portrait of active refusal: fingers in the ears so as not to hear, garments pulled over so as not to see, obstinacy (iṣrār) and pride (istikbār). Qushayrī links the last two: “when their obstinacy lasted, from obstinacy was born their pride” — and he cites: “the time was long upon them, and their hearts hardened.” Hardening is not instantaneous: it is forged by dint of refusing. Each repeated rejection thickens the veil, until the heart can no longer open.

mots.racine: ص ب ع / أ ذ ن
mots.sens: they put their fingers in their ears

mots.racine: غ ش و / ث و ب
mots.sens: they wrapped themselves in their garments

versets.ar: ثُمَّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهَارًا
versets.tr: thumma innī daʿawtuhum jihāran
versets.trad: Then I called them aloud,
versets.commentaire: jihāran (جِهارًا): openly, aloud. In the face of refusal, Noah does not give up but varies his manner: after the ordinary call, the public, declared call. Perseverance is not mechanical repetition but the inventiveness of love: to seek every way to reach the heart. The one who calls for God does not weary at the first failure; he tries again, otherwise.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ إِنِّي أَعْلَنتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا
versets.tr: thumma innī aʿlantu lahum wa-asrartu lahum isrāran
versets.trad: then I made proclamations and spoke to them in secret.
versets.commentaire: aʿlantu… wa-asrartu (أَعْلَنْتُ وَأَسْرَرْتُ): I proclaimed and I spoke in secret. Noah exhausts the modes of the call: openly and confidentially, to the crowd and to each one. Love that wills to save neglects no approach: the strong word that shakes and the gentle word, whispered in the ear, that tames. This very diversity is a mercy: God, through His prophet, knocks at every door of the heart, hoping a single one will open.

mots.sens: and spoke to them in secret

versets.ar: فَقُلْتُ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كَانَ غَفَّارًا
versets.tr: fa-qultu staghfirū rabbakum innahu kāna ghaffāran
versets.trad: I said: "Ask forgiveness of your Lord, for He is the Ever-Forgiving.
versets.commentaire: A key verse, and a pearl of Qushayrī: istaghfirū (اسْتَغْفِروا) — ask forgiveness. “Let the knowers know it: the asking of forgiveness is the knocker at the doors of grace; whoever has a need with God will reach his goal only by first presenting the istighfār.” And he adds: “whoever wishes to obtain the favour, let him take the way of acknowledging the fault (al-ʿudhr wa-l-tanaṣṣul).” Ghaffār — intensive form: He who forgives without ceasing. Before any request, to acknowledge one's lack; the door of bounty opens to whoever knocks in humility.

mots.racine: غ ف ر / ر ب ب

versets.ar: يُرْسِلِ السَّمَاءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا
versets.tr: yursili l-samāʾa ʿalaykum midrāran
versets.trad: He will send down upon you abundant rain;
versets.commentaire: midrār (مِدْرار): abundant, generous — the rain. A surprising promise: to the asking of forgiveness answers not first the heaven of the hereafter, but the heaven of rain that makes the earth fruitful. Qushayrī: “Noah, each time he set out for them more of the ways of good and benefaction, they added to their denial and forgetfulness.” The istighfār opens the floodgates of heaven: the grace of forgiveness pours out in visible benefits. The heart that purifies itself draws upon itself the rain of mercy.

mots.racine: ر س ل / س م و
mots.def: arsala: to send; samāʾ: heaven, here rain.

versets.ar: وَيُمْدِدْكُم بِأَمْوَالٍ وَبَنِينَ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ أَنْهَارًا
versets.tr: wa-yumdidkum bi-amwālin wa-banīna wa-yajʿal lakum jannātin wa-yajʿal lakum anhāran
versets.trad: He will assist you with wealth and children; He will give you gardens and give you rivers.
versets.commentaire: To the istighfār God promises wealth, offspring, gardens, and rivers. All these gifts — goods, children, greenery, waters — are the visible fruits of a reconciled heart. But the sūra will reveal further on (v. 21) that wealth and children can also increase the loss of one who clings to them without God. The same gift is a blessing for the heart turned toward its Lord, a trial for the heart that shuts itself in it. All depends on the orientation.

mots.sens: and He will assist you, supply you

mots.racine: م و ل / ب ن ي

versets.ar: مَّا لَكُمْ لَا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقَارًا
versets.tr: mā lakum lā tarjūna lillāhi waqāran
versets.trad: Why do you not hope, with reverence, for God,
versets.commentaire: lā tarjūna lillāhi waqāran (لا تَرْجونَ لله وَقارًا): you do not expect of God a majesty to revere. Qushayrī glosses: “what is it with you that you do not fear God's greatness? that you do not hope, in revering His command, for a gentleness and a grace?” The waqār is the majesty that calls forth respect: to acknowledge God's greatness is already to bow. The reproach unveils the root of unbelief: not a lack of proofs, but a lack of reverence — the heart that cannot wonder before what is greater than itself.

mots.def: mā lakum: what ails you that.

versets.ar: وَقَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْوَارًا
versets.tr: wa-qad khalaqakum aṭwāran
versets.trad: He who created you in successive stages?
versets.commentaire: aṭwāran (أَطْوارًا): in stages, in successive phases. An argument from within: look at your own formation — drop, clot, embryo, child, adult. He who drew you from nothing through patient degrees well deserves your reverence. The first sign to ponder is not in the heaven but in oneself: each man carries in his own history the proof of a wise Creator, who fashions without haste, stage after stage.

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا
versets.tr: a-lam taraw kayfa khalaqa llāhu sabʿa samāwātin ṭibāqan
versets.trad: Have you not seen how God created seven heavens in layers?
versets.commentaire: sabʿa samāwātin ṭibāqan (سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقًا): seven heavens in layers, in strata. After the inner sign (v. 14), the sign without. Qushayrī: Noah “awakens them to the creation of the heavens and the earth, and to the indications they contain that they are created, and that their Creator deserves the attributes of elevation and might.” The work points to the Worker: the seven-storeyed vault, perfectly ordered, cannot be conceived without a Wisdom that raised it.

mots.racine: س م و / ط ب ق

versets.ar: وَجَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًا وَجَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًا
versets.tr: wa-jaʿala l-qamara fīhinna nūran wa-jaʿala l-shamsa sirājan
versets.trad: He placed the moon among them as a light, and placed the sun as a lamp.
versets.commentaire: al-qamar… nūran wa-l-shams… sirājan (القَمَر نورًا وَالشَّمْس سِراجًا): the moon a light, the sun a lamp. A delicate distinction: the moon is nūr (a soft, reflected light), the sun sirāj (a blazing lamp, source of heat). Two luminaries with distinct roles, attuned to night and day. This ordered harmony of the heavenly bodies is one more sign: nothing is left to chance, each luminary has its measure and its function in the great dwelling of the world.

mots.racine: ق م ر / ن و ر
mots.sens: He placed the moon among them as light

mots.racine: ش م س / س ر ج
mots.sens: and the sun as a lamp

versets.ar: وَاللَّهُ أَنبَتَكُم مِّنَ الْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا
versets.tr: wa-llāhu anbatakum mina l-arḍi nabātan
versets.trad: God has made you grow from the earth like plants.
versets.commentaire: anbatakum mina l-arḍi nabātan (أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الأَرْضِ نَباتًا): He made you grow from the earth like vegetation. A magnificent image: man is planted, he grows, he rises — as the seed becomes a plant. The origin is humble (the earth) but the movement is ascent. The vegetal verb speaks of the slow maturation of being: not a thing manufactured at a stroke, but a living growth, willed and nourished by Him who makes things grow.

mots.racine: أ ر ض / ن ب ت
mots.sens: from the earth, like a plant

versets.ar: ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيهَا وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْرَاجًا
versets.tr: thumma yuʿīdukum fīhā wa-yukhrijukum ikhrājan
versets.trad: Then He will return you to it, and bring you forth again.
versets.commentaire: yuʿīdukum fīhā wa-yukhrijukum (يُعيدُكُمْ فيها وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ): He returns you to it and brings you forth. The complete cycle: from the earth you came, to the earth you return, from the earth you will be drawn again. The same earth that made the plant grow then recalls it will make it green again. The resurrection is but a third movement after birth and death — and the least costly for Him who has already made the first two.

mots.sens: then He returns you to it

versets.ar: وَاللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ بِسَاطًا
versets.tr: wa-llāhu jaʿala lakumu l-arḍa bisāṭan
versets.trad: God has made the earth a carpet for you
versets.commentaire: bisāṭ (بِساط): an outspread carpet. The earth is unrolled beneath man's feet like a carpet beneath the guest one honours. An image of divine hospitality: the world is arranged, welcoming, made to be walked upon and lived in. Each step man sets on the earth should remind him of the delicacy of the One who spread it out for him.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / أ ر ض
mots.sens: God made the earth for you

versets.ar: لِّتَسْلُكُوا مِنْهَا سُبُلًا فِجَاجًا
versets.tr: li-taslukū minhā subulan fijājan
versets.trad: that you may travel its spacious ways.”
versets.commentaire: subulan fijājan (سُبُلًا فِجاجًا): wide roads, spacious passages. The earth-carpet is not closed: it is furrowed with open roads. God has not only prepared the dwelling, He has traced ways in it so that one may move freely. Beyond the letter, an image of life: to man are offered wide roads — provided he chooses to walk them toward his Lord rather than turn in circles in refusal.

mots.racine: س ب ل / ف ج ج

versets.ar: قَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَاتَّبَعُوا مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إِلَّا خَسَارًا
versets.tr: qāla nūḥun rabbi innahum ʿaṣawnī wa-ttabaʿū man lam yazidhu māluhu wa-waladuhu illā khasāran
versets.trad: Noah said further: “My Lord, they have disobeyed me and followed one whose wealth and children only increased his loss.
versets.commentaire: lam yazidhu māluhu wa-waladuhu illā khasāran (لَمْ يَزِدْهُ مالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إلّا خَسارًا): his goods and his children increased him only in loss. A reversal of verse 12: what could be a blessing (wealth, children) becomes ruin for one who clings to it against God. The same gifts increase grace or loss according to the heart. To follow the powerful and the rich who have turned away is to follow blind guides: their very abundance sinks them, and drags down those who imitate them.

mots.racine: ز ي د / م و ل / و ل د
mots.sens: one whose goods and child increased him not

versets.ar: وَمَكَرُوا مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا
versets.tr: wa-makarū makran kubbāran
versets.trad: They schemed a vast scheme
versets.commentaire: makran kubbāran (مَكْرًا كُبّارًا): an immense, monstrous scheme. Qushayrī specifies: it is “their great ones and their rich, those who went astray here below and perished in the hereafter.” The plot is the work of the powerful: they use their influence to turn the weak away. Cunning (makr) is the opposite of the heart's transparency: one manoeuvres in the shadows to preserve one's power against the truth that disturbs.

mots.racine: م ك ر / ك ب ر

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا لَا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا وَلَا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْرًا
versets.tr: wa-qālū lā tadharunna ālihatakum wa-lā tadharunna waddan wa-lā suwāʿan wa-lā yaghūtha wa-yaʿūqa wa-nasran
versets.trad: and said: ‘Do not forsake your gods: forsake not Wadd, nor Suwāʿ, nor Yaghūth, nor Yaʿūq, nor Nasr!’
versets.commentaire: The chiefs' plot takes the form of a call to fidelity to the idols: Wadd, Suwāʿ, Yaghūth, Yaʿūq, Nasr — names of ancient deities. Tradition reports they were first pious men, whose statues were made for remembrance, and which came at last to be worshipped. Idolatry is often born thus: from a legitimate attachment gone astray, from a memory frozen into an idol. The cry “do not forsake your gods!” is that of all conservatisms that prefer the reassuring habit to the disturbing truth.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / أ ل ه

versets.ar: وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا وَلَا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلَالًا
versets.tr: wa-qad aḍallū kathīran wa-lā tazidi l-ẓālimīna illā ḍalālan
versets.trad: Thus they led many astray. But You, increase the wrongdoers only in error!”
versets.commentaire: wa-lā tazidi l-ẓālimīna illā ḍalālan (وَلا تَزِدِ الظّالِمين إلّا ضَلالًا): increase the wrongdoers only in error. A fearsome prayer of Noah. But it states a spiritual law: whoever stubbornly refuses the light sinks all the deeper into the night. Error is not imposed arbitrarily; it is the outcome of a repeated choice to flee. When the heart has locked its door, every call received hardens it the more: refused grace turns into an increase of error.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / ك ث ر
ز ي د / ظ ل م

versets.ar: مِّمَّا خَطِيئَاتِهِمْ أُغْرِقُوا فَأُدْخِلُوا نَارًا فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا لَهُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ أَنصَارًا
versets.tr: mimmā khaṭīʾātihim ughriqū fa-udkhilū nāran fa-lam yajidū lahum min dūni llāhi anṣāran
versets.trad: Because of their faults they were drowned, then made to enter a Fire, and they found no helpers besides God.
versets.commentaire: min dūni llāhi anṣāran (مِنْ دونِ الله أَنْصارًا): no helpers besides God. A striking contrast: drowned by water, then made to enter fire — the water of chastisement here below, the fire of the hereafter. And at the heart of the verse, the lesson: they found no protector besides God. The idols invoked (v. 23), the wealth and children (v. 21), the powerful followed — nothing helped them. On the day of distress, every support other than God proves vain. The One they fled was the only refuge.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ن و ر
mots.sens: then made to enter a fire
mots.def: adkhala: to make enter; nār: fire.

mots.ar: فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا لَهُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ أَنصَارًا
mots.tr: fa-lam yajidū lahum min dūni llāhi anṣāran
mots.racine: و ج د / ن ص ر
mots.sens: they found no helpers besides God

versets.ar: وَقَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّارًا
versets.tr: wa-qāla nūḥun rabbi lā tadhar ʿalā l-arḍi mina l-kāfirīna dayyāran
versets.trad: Noah said: “My Lord! Leave not on the earth a single dweller among the deniers.
versets.commentaire: lā tadhar… dayyāran (لا تَذَرْ دَيّارًا): leave not a single dweller. Qushayrī specifies the context: this prayer came “after God had made known to him that none of your people will believe but the one who has already believed.” Noah does not curse out of spite: he prays after a divine revelation about the outcome. The prophet's prayer espouses God's knowledge; it does not outrun His decree but accords with it. It is Noah's long patience that, at the end, surrenders entirely to what God has shown him.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / و ذ ر
ك ف ر / د و ر
mots.sens: of the deniers, a single dweller

versets.ar: إِنَّكَ إِن تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبَادَكَ وَلَا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا
versets.tr: innaka in tadharhum yuḍillū ʿibādaka wa-lā yalidū illā fājiran kaffāran
versets.trad: If You spare them, they will lead Your servants astray and will beget only depraved deniers.
versets.commentaire: lā yalidū illā fājiran kaffāran (لا يَلِدوا إلّا فاجِرًا كَفّارًا): they will beget only the depraved and the great deniers. Noah's argument is that of contagion: left alive, they would lead God's servants astray and pass their denial on to their offspring. Error is not only personal: it spreads, is transmitted, becomes an inheritance. The evil that takes root in a heart seeks to extend itself. But this very gravity recalls, conversely, the responsibility to transmit the good.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / ع ب د
mots.sens: they will lead Your servants astray
mots.def: aḍalla: to lead astray; ʿibād: servants.

mots.racine: و ل د / ف ج ر / ك ف ر
mots.sens: and beget only depraved, great deniers

versets.ar: رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِنًا وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ وَلَا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا تَبَارًا
versets.tr: rabbi ghfir lī wa-li-wālidayya wa-li-man dakhala baytiya muʾminan wa-li-l-muʾminīna wa-l-muʾmināti wa-lā tazidi l-ẓālimīna illā tabāran
versets.trad: My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house as a believer, and the believing men and women. And to the wrongdoers, increase only ruin!”
versets.commentaire: A closing verse: Noah's prayer widens into circles of love. He asks forgiveness for himself, for his parents, for whoever enters his house as a believer, then for all believing men and women, to the end of time. After a life of refused calling, the prophet does not close in on bitterness: he opens his heart to the whole community of the faithful, present and to come. Such is the hidden fruitfulness of patience: the one who was scarcely heard prays for multitudes he will never see. His narrow house becomes, through prayer, the house of all believers.

mots.racine: غ ف ر / و ل د
mots.def: ghafara: to forgive; wālidayn: the two parents.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ب ي ت / أ م ن
mots.sens: and whoever enters my house a believer
and the believing men and women

mots.racine: ز ي د / ظ ل م / ت ب ر
mots.sens: increase the wrongdoers only in ruin
